title: Nb$_3$Sn Thin Films Using a Cu-Sn Route for Dark Matter Detection
abstract: Axion dark matter searches require superconducting radio-frequency (SRF) cavities on copper (Cu) substrates with quality factors Q > 10^5 in multi-tesla magnetic fields. Copper reduces thermal noise and allows complex geometries. Nb3Sn is a strong candidate due to its superior superconducting properties. However, uniform high-Tc Nb3Sn thin films on Cu are challenging due to Sn loss and substrate strain.   This work uses solid-state diffusion of Sn from high-Sn Cu-Sn alloys into Nb layers to form Nb3Sn at Cu-compatible temperatures (650-750°C), avoiding the traditional ~1100°C vapor method. Varying Cu-Sn composition yielded an optimal alloy that maintains high Sn activity. Compositional and thermal expansion analyses showed Tc is suppressed below 18 K by Cu substrate strain. Experiments on Nb and sapphire substrates isolated the strain effects. Two routes were developed: (1) Cu-Sn on Ta-coated Cu with hot Nb sputtering (Tc = 16 K), and (2) Nb on Ta/Cu with Cu-Sn evaporation and ex-situ reaction. Route 2 gave uniform Nb3Sn and was chosen for cavity coating. A hexagonal cavity combining designs from the University of Washington and Center for Axion and Precision Physics was coated using Route 2 and tested to 50 mK and 9 T. At zero field it reached Q = 77,000 (40% above bare Cu's Q = 55,000), but Q dropped sharply in field. Nb3Sn coatings on Cu cavities outperform bare Cu at zero field and provide practical routes for improved axion detectors.
summary: This paper presents a new method for coating copper cavities with Nb3Sn using a low-temperature solid-state diffusion process. This approach avoids the high temperatures that typically cause tin loss and substrate damage. The resulting coatings improve the quality factor of the cavities at zero field, providing a practical advancement for axion dark matter detectors.
main_problem: Developing a method to create uniform, high-Tc Nb3Sn thin films on copper substrates for axion dark matter detection, overcoming challenges like Sn loss and substrate-induced strain.
main_result: A solid-state diffusion route (Route 2) was developed that produces uniform Nb3Sn coatings on Cu cavities, achieving a quality factor of Q = 77,000 at zero field, which outperforms bare copper.
method: The researchers used solid-state diffusion of Sn from high-Sn Cu-Sn alloys into Nb layers at temperatures between 650-750°C and compared two different coating routes.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of Nb3Sn thin films deposited on copper (Cu) substrates, specifically applied to a hexagonal superconducting radio-frequency (SRF) cavity designed for axion detection.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), quality factor (Q), and magnetic field dependence of Q.
important_parameters: Temperatures of 650-750°C, magnetic fields up to 9 T, and temperatures as low as 50 mK.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the strain-induced suppression of Tc can be isolated by comparing Nb and sapphire substrates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for Nb3Sn on Cu for axion searches, describes the solid-state diffusion method and alloy composition, compares two coating routes, analyzes the impact of substrate strain on Tc, and concludes with the performance testing of a coated hexagonal cavity.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
